findings.title: Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider
findings.summary: Summary The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is releasing this advisory in response to ransomware actors leveraging unpatched instances of a vulnerability in SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider. This incident reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched versions of SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. SimpleHelp versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-57727 —a path traversal vulnerability. 1 Ransomware actors likely leveraged CVE-2024-57727 to access downstream customers’ unpatched SimpleHelp RMM for disruption of services in double extortion compromises. 1 CISA added CVE-2024-57727 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on Feb. 13, 2025. CISA urges software vendors, downstream customers, and end users to immediately implement the Mitigations listed in this advisory based on confirmed compromise or risk of compromise. Download the PDF version of this report: AA25-163A Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider (PDF, 420.49 KB ) Mitigations CISA recommends organizations implement the mitigations below to respond to emerging ransomware activity exploiting SimpleHelp software. These mitigations align with the Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals (CPGs) developed by CISA and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). The CPGs provide a minimum set of practices and protections that CISA and NIST recommend all organizations implement. CISA and NIST based the CPGs on existing cybersecurity frameworks and guidance to protect against the most common and impactful threats, tactics, techniques, and procedures. Visit CISA’s CPGs webpage for more information on the CPGs, including additional recommended baseline protections. These mitigations apply to all critical infrastructure

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2024-57727 is an unauthenticated path traversal vulnerability (CWE-22) in the SimpleHelp RMM web application's toolbox/file-download functionality. The application fails to adequately sanitize user-supplied path input, enabling remote unauthenticated attackers to craft HTTP requests containing directory-traversal sequences (e.g., '../') to download arbitrary files from the SimpleHelp host. Sensitive files exposed include server configuration files (serverconfig.xml) containing plaintext/hashed credentials, hashed user passwords, and—on Linux systems—system files such as /etc/passwd and SSH private keys (e.g., /root/.ssh/id_rsa). The vulnerability is network-exploitable, requires no authentication and no user interaction, and results in high confidentiality impact with no integrity or availability impact. It was part of a chainable set of three SimpleHelp CVEs disclosed in January 2025.
enrichment.affected_products: SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) versions 5.5.7 and earlier (v5.4.x line patched via v5.4.10)
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public exploit and weaponized modules exist, including a Metasploit module (auxiliary/scanner/http/simplehelp_toolbox_path_traversal) published by Rapid7 (https://www.rapid7.com/db/modules/auxiliary/scanner/http/simplehelp_toolbox_path_traversal/) and a PoC walkthrough by OffSec (https://www.offsec.com/blog/cve-2024-57727/).
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — SimpleHelp released version 5.5.8 on January 10, 2025, which patches CVE-2024-57727 (along with CVE-2024-57726 and CVE-2024-57728). For the v5.4.x branch, version 5.4.10 is the patched release. Available at https://simple-help.com/downloads. Announcement: https://community.simple-help.com/t/simplehelp-5-5-8-critical-security-fixes/1570. Vendor advisory: https://simple-help.com/blogs/security-vulnerabilities-in-simplehelp-5-5-7-and-earlier-what-you-need-to-know
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild. CISA published Advisory AA25-163A on June 12, 2025 documenting DragonForce ransomware actors exploiting CVE-2024-57727 against unpatched SimpleHelp RMM instances to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider, conducting a double-extortion ransomware attack. This reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. Sources: CISA Advisory AA25-163A (https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-163a); SecurityWeek (https://www.securityweek.com/simplehelp-vulnerability-exploited-against-utility-billing-software-users/); Picus Security (https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/ransomware-actors-exploit-cve-2024-57727-in-unpatched-simplehelp-rmm); Industrial Cyber (https://industrialcyber.co/cisa/cisa-flags-exploitation-of-simplehelp-rmm-vulnerability-in-ransomware-attacks-since-january/). CVE-2024-57727 was added to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog in February 2025.
enrichment.threat_actors: DragonForce ransomware actors (per CISA Advisory AA25-163A)
enrichment.mitigation: Upgrade SimpleHelp server to version 5.5.8 or later (latest available version recommended); for the v5.4.x branch, apply the v5.4.10 patch instead. Additional hardening: enable second-factor authentication on all SimpleHelp accounts, audit deployed Remote Access Services to confirm patching, add SimpleHelp binaries to endpoint security allowlists where required, monitor for and remove suspicious three-letter-named executables (e.g., aaa.exe, bbb.exe) created after January 2025, and ensure the RMM server is not internet-facing. SimpleHelp states that keeping the server up to date is the only required action.

findings.title: AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web
findings.summary: Posted by Thomas Brunner, Yu-Han Liu, Moni Pande At Google, our Threat Intelligence teams are dedicated to staying ahead of real-world adversarial activity, proactively monitoring emerging threats before they can impact users. Right now, Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a top priority for the security community, anticipating it as a primary attack vector for adversaries to target and compromise AI agents. But while the danger of IPI is widely discussed, are threat actors actually exploiting this vector today – and if so, how? To answer these questions and to uncover real-world abuse, we initiated a broad sweep of the public web to monitor for known indirect prompt injection patterns. This is what we found. The threat of indirect prompt injection Unlike a direct injection where a user "jailbreaks" a chatbot, IPI occurs when an AI system processes content—like a website, email, or document—that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this poisoned content, it may silently follow the attacker's commands instead of the user's original intent. This is not a new area of concern for us and Google has been working tirelessly to combat these threats. Our efforts involve cross-functional collaboration between researchers at Google DeepMind (GDM) and defenders like the Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG). We have previously detailed our work in this area and researchers have further highlighted the evolving nature of these vulnerabilities. Despite this collective focus, a fundamental question remains: to what degree are real-world malicious actors currently operationalizing these attacks? Proactive monitoring at Google The landscape of IPI on the web There are many channels through which attackers might try to send prompt injections. However, one location is particularly easy to observe - the public web. Here, threat actors may simply seed prompt injections on websites in hope of corrupting AI systems that browse them. Public research confirms these attacks ar

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) occurs when an LLM/AI agent ingests untrusted external content (websites, emails, documents, calendar invites, image alt-text, chat messages, payment flows, retrieved documents) poisoned with hidden or overt instructions. The model cannot reliably separate untrusted user/tool data from trusted system instructions, so it follows injected instructions as if they were legitimate commands. Per the primary Google blog and corroborating research (Forcepoint, Unit42, HelpNetSecurity), delivery techniques include: invisible text via CSS ('display:none', 1px font, transparent color, off-screen positioning), white text on white backgrounds, HTML comments, 'aria-hidden' / 'visually-hidden' accessibility classes, custom metadata namespaces (e.g., 'ai:action' meta tags), HTML/SVG CDATA encapsulation, invisible Unicode characters, multi-language / multi-step payloads, terminal-command injection ('sudo rm -rf'), OAuth/tool-call chaining via MCP tool descriptions, and adaptive/streaming payloads. Realized goals observed include data exfiltration to attacker-controlled URLs, financial transaction redirection (PayPal/Stripe donation scams), unauthorized file deletion, AI ad-review evasion (Unit42 case against reviewerpress[.]com), tone override, prompt-token seizure for DoS, brand hijack / SEO manipulation, and CVSS-9-class zero-click exfiltration of tenant data in Copilot-class agents.
enrichment.affected_products: Indirect Prompt Injection as a class affects AI assistants and agents broadly. Specifically affected or named in/around this report: Google Gemini app, Gemini 2.5 models, Workspace with Gemini (Gmail, Docs), Google AI Overviews / Search, Common Crawl corpus; Microsoft 365 Copilot (related CVE-2025-32711 'EchoLeak'); ChatGPT (chatgpt.com); Google Gemini Enterprise (GeminiJack zero-click by Noma Labs); Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools/servers in agentic stacks including Copilot Studio, Azure AI Foundry, and Dataverse MCP. Specific version ranges were not enumerated in the Google blog.
enrichment.exploit_available:
Yes — multiple public PoCs and weaponized exploit code exist for the broader Indirect Prompt Injection class:
• Google AI Vulnerability Reward Program PoC rules (demonstrates IPIs that summarize a victim's email and exfiltrate to attacker-controlled accounts): https://bughunters.google.com/about/rules/google-friends/ai-vulnerability-reward-program-rules
• EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711) zero-click PoC against Microsoft 365 Copilot by Aim Labs: https://www.aim.security/lp/aim-labs-echoleak-m365
• ChatGPhish PoC against ChatGPT (Andi Ahmeti, The Register 2026-05-29): https://www.theregister.com/research/2026/05/29/chatgpt-prompt-injection-turns-web-pages-into-phishing-lures/5248137
• SafeBreach Gemini voice-assistant PoC (2026-06-03): https://www.safebreach.com/blog/gemini-voice-assistant-prompt-injection-exploit/
• Miggo Gemini calendar-invite semantic attack PoC (2026-01-19): https://www.miggo.io/post/weaponizing-calendar-invites-a-semantic-attack-on-google-gemini
• Atlas prompt-injection PoC: https://github.com/brennanbrown/atlas-prompt-injection-poc
• PayloadsAllTheThings Prompt Injection payloads: https://github.com/swisskyrepo/PayloadsAllTheThings/blob/master/Prompt%20Injection/README.md
Google also observed raw IPI payloads in the live web via Common Crawl (Nov 2025–Feb 2026) with a 'relative increase of 32% in the malicious category'.
enrichment.patch_available: No single patch is associated with this Google blog because it is a class-level threat report rather than a CVE advisory. Google describes a continuous hardening program (adversarial training of Gemini 2.5, new classifier iterations, additional prompt-injection defenses deployed 'later this year' as quoted in Google's layered-defense blog), overseen via the AI Vulnerability Reward Program (https://bughunters.google.com/about/rules/google-friends/ai-vulnerability-reward-program-rules). For related, specific IPI bugs disclosed publicly: Microsoft 365 Copilot was server-side patched against CVE-2025-32711 'EchoLeak' (disclosed by Aim Labs June 2025; Microsoft advisory https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2025-32711 / NVD https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2025-32711).
enrichment.active_exploitation:
Yes — independently confirmed by multiple sources:
• Google Threat Intelligence Group (this blog, 2026-04-23): live observation of IPI in the Common Crawl corpus with a 'relative increase of 32% in the malicious category between November 2025 and February 2026.'
• Forcepoint X-Labs (2026-04-22): documented 10 IPI payloads captured in the wild, including PayPal transaction hijack and Stripe donation redirection — https://www.forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads
• Palo Alto Unit42 (2026-03-03): documented 'reviewerpress[.]com' attack against AI ad-moderation systems and broader IPI activity against AI agents — https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection
• HelpNetSecurity (2026-04-24): anonymized PayPal/Stripe donation scams and AI-mediated financial abuse observed in the wild — https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2026/04/24/indirect-prompt-injection-in-the-wild/
• The Register / Andi Ahmeti (2026-05-29): ChatGPhish PoC transforms ChatGPT summarization into phishing link delivery with QR-code pivot — https://www.theregister.com/research/2026/05/29/chatgpt-prompt-injection-turns-web-pages-into-phishing-lures/5248137
• Microsoft 365 Copilot (EchoLeak, CVE-2025-32711): disclosed June 2025 but Microsoft has stated no in-the-wild exploitation at disclosure time per Sentra reporting.
• Additional disclosed research-stage exploits: Gemini calendar-invite semantic attack (Miggo, 2026-01-19), GeminiJack zero-click in Gemini Enterprise (Noma Labs), Gemini voice-assistant IPI via IM apps (SafeBreach, 2026-06-03).
enrichment.threat_actors: None known (specific APT groups or ransomware operators were not named in this report). The Google Threat Intelligence Group identifies only opportunistic 'website authors using Common Crawl to seed prompt injections on websites' across categories of pranks, SEO manipulation, AI-agent deterrence, data exfiltration, and data destruction. Microsoft's companion post references 'threat actor-controlled endpoint' and 'upstream maintainer' risks generically without naming specific APTs.
enrichment.mitigation:
Google's mitigation posture (per the report and Google's layered-defense / Workspace mitigation blogs) is defense-in-depth with no single fix. Key elements:
• AI red-teaming (human + automated); Google AI Vulnerability Reward Program (announced 2025-10-06); centralized vulnerability catalog.
• Adversarial training and model hardening of Gemini 2.5.
• Purpose-built ML content classifiers for prompt injection (Workspace + Gemini app).
• System-level safeguards including 'security thought reinforcement' and instruction hierarchy.
• Markdown/HTML sanitization and 'suspicious URL redaction' before model/agent consumption.
• Unsafe-URL detection backed by Google Safe Browsing.
• User confirmation framework before high-impact agent actions.
• End-user security/mitigation notifications.
• Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approval for agentic actions.
• Synthetic-data augmentation via the Simula framework (+75% coverage reported).
• Deterministic Policy Engine: tool-chaining policies, regex takedowns, user-confirmation gates.
• Workspace hardening: synthetic IPI corpora, dataset augmentation, prompt-level re-instruction.

Complementary guidance from HelpNetSecurity and Microsoft: enforce a strict data/instruction boundary; treat all model output as untrusted; sandbox rendering of model output; isolate tool execution; provide AI kill-switches and DLP for agent outputs; limit agent autonomy ('least agency'); audit agent telemetry; apply human-in-the-loop approval in Copilot Studio and Microsoft Purview DLP. Microsoft Prompt Shields / Azure AI Content Safety / Defender for Cloud AI workload protection are referenced for inspecting tool metadata.

findings.title: Google Workspace’s continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an evolving threat vector targeting users of complex AI applications with multiple data sources, such as Workspace with Gemini. This technique enables the attacker to influence the behavior of an LLM by injecting malicious instructions into the data or tools used by the LLM as it completes the user’s query. This may even be possible without any input directly from the user. IPI is not the kind of technical problem you “solve” and move on. Sophisticated LLMs with increasing use of agentic automation combined with a wide range of content create an ultra-dynamic and evolving playground for adversarial attacks. That’s why Google takes a sophisticated and comprehensive approach to these attacks. We’re continuously improving LLM resistance to IPI attacks and launching AI application capabilities with ever-improving defenses. Staying ahead of the latest indirect prompt injection attacks is critical to our mission of securing Workspace with Gemini. In our previous blog “ Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy ”, we reviewed the layered architecture of our IPI defenses. In this blog, we’ll share more detail on the continuous approach we take to improve these defenses and to solve for new attacks. New attack discovery By proactively discovering and cataloging new attack vectors through internal and external programs, we can identify vulnerabilities and deploy robust defenses ahead of adversarial activity. Human Red-Teaming Human Red-Teaming uses adversarial simulations to uncover security and safety vulnerabilities. Specialized teams execute attacks based on realistic user profiles to exploit weaknesses, coordinating with product teams to resolve identified issues. Automated Red-Teaming Automated Red-Teaming is done via dynamic, machine-learning-driven frameworks to stress-test environments. By algorithmically generating and iterating on attack payloads, we can mi

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is a class of attack against LLM-backed applications (e.g., Workspace with Gemini) where an adversary embeds malicious natural-language instructions inside a data source or tool that the LLM consumes while completing the user's request (e.g., an email body, Google Doc/Slide, Drive file, web page, or a Cloud log line). The LLM treats those untrusted instructions as legitimate directives, causing it to ignore the user's intent and instead (a) generate attacker-controlled output (phishing messages, malicious URLs, fake security alerts), (b) take unauthorized actions through connected tools (sending mail, modifying Drive/Docs assets, invoking Cloud integrations), or (c) exfiltrate conversation or workspace data. Concretely demonstrated techniques include hidden HTML/CSS (zero-sized text, display:none, opacity:0, color camouflage), SVG/CDATA cloaking, JavaScript-rendered prompts, and URL fragment (#) injection (e.g., 'HashJack'). Per Google's blog, IPI can succeed even without any direct user input, and it is amplified by agentic automation that combines many tools and data sources.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Workspace with Gemini (Gemini app, plus Gemini integrations in Gmail, Google Docs, Google Slides, Google Drive, and Google Chat); Gemini 2.5 models; Google Cloud Gemini Cloud Assist. (Indirect prompt injection is a class of attack against AI applications with multiple data sources and agentic tooling; beyond Workspace, it broadly impacts LLM-backed agents that ingest untrusted data from web pages, logs, and third-party tools.)
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public proof-of-concept exploits exist: (1) HiddenLayer (Sep 25, 2024) published a working PoC that injects prompt-injection content (including fake 'System:' tokens and Arabic-unicode-obfuscated URLs) into emails, Slides speaker notes, and Drive documents to hijack Gemini in Workspace and produce phishing output. Source: https://www.hiddenlayer.com/research/new-gemini-for-workspace-vulnerability-enabling-phishing-content-manipulation (2) Tenable (TRA-2025-10) published step-by-step reproduction instructions for injecting malicious prompts via GCP log contents (e.g., User-Agent HTTP header) into Gemini Cloud Assist. Source: https://www.tenable.com/security/research/tra-2025-10 Weaponized/exploited examples observed in the wild are also documented by Palo Alto Unit 42 (see 'active_exploitation').
enrichment.patch_available: No single traditional vendor patch is associated with IPI itself because it is a class of attack addressed through continuous, ongoing hardening of Workspace with Gemini, Gemini, and Gemini 2.5, as described by the reference Google blog and its companion layered-defense post (http://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html). Specific product-level defects are addressed individually — for example, Tenable TRA-2025-10 in Gemini Cloud Assist was fixed by Google (hyperlink rendering removed from log-summarization responses), CVE-2025-54131 affecting Cursor < 1.3 was fixed in Cursor 1.3, and Gemini-in-Workspace behaviors reported by HiddenLayer were followed by Google's mitigations (the bug was reported and ultimately addressed rather than land as a CVE). Reports can be filed via Google's AI Vulnerability Reward Program: https://bughunters.google.com/about/rules/google-friends/5238081279623168/abuse-vulnerability-reward-program-rules
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes. In-the-wild exploitation is confirmed. Palo Alto Unit 42 (March 2026 reporting covering observations from December 2025) documents that web-based indirect prompt injection is 'actively weaponized in the wild.' A specific documented campaign embedded hidden IPI instructions in a scam 'military glasses' advertorial hosted at hxxps[:]//reviewerpress[.]com/advertorial-maxvision-can/?lang=en to trick AI-based ad-review systems into approving scam content. Google's own companion materials (the April 2, 2026 blog and the June 2025 'Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy' post — http://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html) explicitly cite real-world exploit patterns used to train IPI classifiers, including chatbot hijacking that exposed internal information, summarizer-driven unauthorized actions such as rogue email sending, and data exfiltration via infected files. Earlier, HiddenLayer publicly demonstrated a working Gemini-in-Workspace IPI PoC in September 2024. Sources: https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/ ; http://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html ; https://www.hiddenlayer.com/research/new-gemini-for-workspace-vulnerability-enabling-phishing-content-manipulation
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. Neither the Google blog, Google's companion layered-defense post, nor the Google Workspace admin knowledge article name any specific APT group, nation-state actor, or ransomware operator. Public reporting (Palo Alto Unit 42) attributes observed in-the-wild IPI campaigns to opportunistic, financially motivated actors (e.g., ad-fraud operators) rather than to a tracked threat-actor group.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's stated mitigation strategy is layered rather than a single patch: (1) Deterministic / system-level defenses: markdown sanitization, suspicious-URL redaction via Google Safe Browsing, a User-Confirmation framework that requires explicit user approval for risky operations, tool-chaining policies, and a centralized Policy Engine that performs regex-based takedowns of known-bad patterns. (2) ML-based defenses: proprietary prompt-injection content classifiers trained on synthetic data generated via the Simula harness and on real-world samples. (3) LLM-based defenses: targeted security 'thought reinforcement' — instructions appended around prompt content to steer Gemini to ignore adversarial commands. (4) Model hardening: adversarial robustness testing and adversarial-data retraining of Gemini 2.5. (5) End-user mitigations: security notifications surfaced to users when IPI is detected and contained. Google also augments these defenses via its AI Vulnerability Reward Program (e.g., a $15,000 prompt-injection bug bounty paid in March 2026, indicating third-party reporting also feeds the defense pipeline). For specific related CVEs chained with IPI (e.g., CVE-2025-54131 in Cursor version < 1.3), vendors have shipped dedicated version fixes.

findings.title: Architecting Security for Agentic Capabilities in Chrome
findings.summary: Posted by Nathan Parker, Chrome security team Chrome has been advancing the web’s security for well over 15 years, and we’re committed to meeting new challenges and opportunities with AI. Billions of people trust Chrome to keep them safe by default, and this is a responsibility we take seriously. Following the recent launch of Gemini in Chrome and the preview of agentic capabilities , we want to share our approach and some new innovations to improve the safety of agentic browsing. The primary new threat facing all agentic browsers is indirect prompt injection . It can appear in malicious sites, third-party content in iframes, or from user-generated content like user reviews, and can cause the agent to take unwanted actions such as initiating financial transactions or exfiltrating sensitive data. Given this open challenge, we are investing in a layered defense that includes both deterministic and probabilistic defenses to make it difficult and costly for attackers to cause harm. Designing safe agentic browsing for Chrome has involved deep collaboration of security experts across Google. We built on Gemini's existing protections and agent security principles and have implemented several new layers for Chrome. We’re introducing a user alignment critic where the agent’s actions are vetted by a separate model that is isolated from untrusted content. We’re also extending Chrome’s origin-isolation capabilities to constrain what origins the agent can interact with, to just those that are relevant to the task. Our layered defense also includes user confirmations for critical steps, real-time detection of threats , and red-teaming and response . We’ll step through these layers below. Checking agent outputs with User Alignment Critic The main planning model for Gemini uses page content shared in Chrome to decide what action to take next. Exposure to untrusted web content means it is inherently vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. We use techniques like spotlighting that di

enrichment.technical_details: Insufficient policy enforcement in the WebView tag of Google Chrome (CWE-862 Missing Authorization) prior to version 143.0.7499.192 allows an attacker who convinces a user to install a malicious Chrome extension to inject scripts or HTML into a privileged Chrome page. In the context of Chrome's agentic Gemini panel / WebView process, this can be abused via declarativeNetRequest (DNR) permissions to perform prompt-extraction / prompt-engineering attacks against Gemini, leak PII, gain unrestricted camera/microphone access, and reach local files for privacy invasion (Chromium issue 463155954). It falls into the broader class of indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities in agentic browsers described in the referenced Google Security Blog post.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Chrome prior to 143.0.7499.192 (Windows, macOS, Linux) and other Chromium-based browsers
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. A public proof-of-concept is available at https://github.com/fevar54/CVE-2026-0628-POC [7]. The PoC demonstrates injection of scripts into privileged Chrome pages (including the new Gemini panel) via the <webview> tag from a malicious Chrome extension (CWE-862 Missing Authorization).
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Google shipped a fix in Chrome Stable channel update 143.0.7499.192 for Windows/Mac/Linux on January 6, 2026 (https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2026/01/stable-channel-update-for-desktop.html); the underlying Chromium bug is tracked at https://issues.chromium.org/issues/463155954.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No known active exploitation in the wild has been reported. As of January 2026, NVD (https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-0628) and OffSeq Radar (https://radar.offseq.com/threat/cve-2026-0628-insufficient-policy-enforcement-in-g-6d67a575) both report no known in-the-wild exploitation. Unit 42's write-up of CVE-2026-0628 likewise notes no in-the-wild attacks, calling out that Google quickly patched the flaw.
enrichment.mitigation: Update Google Chrome (and other Chromium-based browsers such as Microsoft Edge) to version 143.0.7499.192 or later. Defense-in-depth guidance from the LinkedIn vendor blog includes: an isolated User Alignment Critic that vets sensitive actions; Agent Origin Sets that split accessible origins into read-only vs. read-writable sets to limit blast radius; explicit user confirmations for sensitive sites (banking, healthcare), Google Password Manager sign-ins, and consequential actions such as payments; a prompt-injection classifier integrated with Safe Browsing; 'Spotlighting' to make the model prioritize system/user instructions over page content; transparency work logs; and continuous red-teaming.

findings.title: Rust in Android: move fast and fix things
findings.summary: Posted by Jeff Vander Stoep, Android Last year, we wrote about why a memory safety strategy that focuses on vulnerability prevention in new code quickly yields durable and compounding gains. This year we look at how this approach isn’t just fixing things, but helping us move faster . The 2025 data continues to validate the approach, with memory safety vulnerabilities falling below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time. Updated data for 2025. This data covers first-party and third-party (open source) code changes to the Android platform across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust. This post is published a couple of months before the end of 2025, but Android’s industry-standard 90-day patch window means that these results are very likely close to final. We can and will accelerate patching when necessary. We adopted Rust for its security and are seeing a 1000x reduction in memory safety vulnerability density compared to Android’s C and C++ code . But the biggest surprise was Rust's impact on software delivery. With Rust changes having a 4x lower rollback rate and spending 25% less time in code review , the safer path is now also the faster one. In this post, we dig into the data behind this shift and also cover: How we’re expanding our reach: We're pushing to make secure code the default across our entire software stack. We have updates on Rust adoption in first-party apps, the Linux kernel, and firmware. Our first rust memory safety vulnerability...almost: We'll analyze a near-miss memory safety bug in unsafe Rust: how it happened, how it was mitigated, and steps we're taking to prevent recurrence. It’s also a good chance to answer the question “if Rust can have memory safety issues, why bother at all?” Building Better Software, Faster Developing an operating system requires the low-level control and predictability of systems programming languages like C, C++, and Rust. While Java and Kotlin are important for Android platform development, their role is complementa

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2025-48530 is a linear/heap buffer overflow (out-of-bounds access, CWE-125) in the CrabbyAVIF AVIF image parser/decoder (AOSP bug A-419563680). In multiple locations there is a possible condition that results in OOB accesses due to an incorrect bounds check — affecting YUV planes (alpha, Y, and UV planes), chroma width calculation, plane size calculation, and row bytes when decoding crafted AVIF images. A remote attacker could deliver a malicious AVIF (e.g., via a web page, messaging app, or other image-rendering surface) to trigger the OOB access in the System component, potentially leading to remote code execution when chained with other vulnerabilities, with no additional execution privileges required (severity Critical in Google's bulletin). The bug originated from an unsafe Rust code block in the parser, marking the first Rust-based memory-safety vulnerability discovered in Android.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Android System component (Android 16 and earlier versions), specifically the CrabbyAVIF AVIF image parser/decoder shipped in AOSP / Pixel devices.
enrichment.exploit_available: No public proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit is publicly available. The vulnerability was identified and patched before ever reaching a public Android release; Google's blog post notes that Android's Scudo hardened allocator 'deterministically rendered this vulnerability non-exploitable' due to guard pages surrounding secondary allocations.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — patched in the Android Security Bulletin—August 2025 (Security Patch Level 2025-08-05), published at https://source.android.com/docs/security/bulletin/2025-08-01. The fix is also reflected in subsequent Android Security Bulletins (the issue was addressed before reaching any public release).
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed active exploitation in the wild. According to Google's November 13, 2025 Security Blog post ('Rust in Android: move fast and fix things'), the very first Rust-based memory-safety vulnerability — a linear buffer overflow in CrabbyAVIF (CVE-2025-48530) — was discovered and fixed before being shipped to a public Android release, and the Scudo hardened allocator deterministically rendered the bug non-exploitable. No public reporting from CISA, Google Threat Intelligence Group, or commercial spyware trackers has confirmed in-the-wild use of this specific CVE.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply the Android Security Patch Level 2025-08-05 (Android Security Bulletin—August 2025). Android's Scudo hardened allocator (default on Google Pixel devices and many other Android partners) deterministically mitigates exploitation of this OOB access by surrounding secondary allocations with guard pages. Additional hardening: keep devices updated to the latest Android Security Patch Level, enable Google Play Protect for runtime threat detection, and use mobile threat defense (MTD) tools for layered protection. Google is also working with OEM partners to make Scudo mandatory across all Android devices.

findings.title: Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team With the rapid adoption of generative AI, a new wave of threats is emerging across the industry with the aim of manipulating the AI systems themselves. One such emerging attack vector is indirect prompt injections. Unlike direct prompt injections, where an attacker directly inputs malicious commands into a prompt, indirect prompt injections involve hidden malicious instructions within external data sources. These may include emails, documents, or calendar invites that instruct AI to exfiltrate user data or execute other rogue actions. As more governments, businesses, and individuals adopt generative AI to get more done, this subtle yet potentially potent attack becomes increasingly pertinent across the industry, demanding immediate attention and robust security measures. At Google, our teams have a longstanding precedent of investing in a defense-in-depth strategy, including robust evaluation, threat analysis, AI security best practices , AI red-teaming , adversarial training, and model hardening for generative AI tools. This approach enables safer adoption of Gemini in Google Workspace and the Gemini app (we refer to both in this blog as “Gemini” for simplicity). Below we describe our prompt injection mitigation product strategy based on extensive research, development, and deployment of improved security mitigations. A layered security approach Google has taken a layered security approach introducing security measures designed for each stage of the prompt lifecycle. From Gemini 2.5 model hardening, to purpose-built machine learning (ML) models detecting malicious instructions, to system-level safeguards, we are meaningfully elevating the difficulty, expense, and complexity faced by an attacker. This approach compels adversaries to resort to methods that are either more easily identified or demand greater resources. Our model training with adversarial data significantly enhanced our defenses against indirect prompt

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks exploit the architecture of generative AI assistants like Gemini, which ingest external/untrusted content (emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages) as part of fulfilling user requests. Attackers embed hidden natural-language instructions within that untrusted content (e.g., in a calendar event description, HTML email with hidden text, or speaker notes in a document). When Gemini processes this content, the adversarial instructions are concatenated with the legitimate system/user prompt, causing the model to follow attacker-dictated actions instead of the user's genuine intent. Documented attack goals include: data exfiltration from the user's inbox/files via attacker-controlled URLs or markdown image links, unauthorized use of connected tools (e.g., Calendar.create from a malicious invite), unauthorized deletion of events, control of smart-home devices via Gemini Voice Assistant, and 'memory poisoning' of long-term Gemini memory. This is not a traditional software memory-safety bug; it is an inherent property of LLM agents that treat retrieved data as contextually trusted.
enrichment.affected_products: Gemini in Google Workspace (Gmail, Docs, Drive, Chat), Gemini app, Gemini 2.5 models (including Gemini 2.5-Flash, Gemini 2.0, and Gemini 1.5 Flash/Pro), Gemini CLI
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public PoCs exist: (1) SafeBreach Labs demonstration of Gemini Voice Assistant exploitation via 'Fake Context Alignment' through messaging app notifications; (2) Miggo Research 'Semantic Attack' PoC using malicious Google Calendar invite descriptions to trigger unauthorized Calendar actions; (3) Google/DeepMind academic research (arxiv 2505.14534) on indirect prompt injection against Gemini. Additionally, the related EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711) zero-click attack against Microsoft 365 Copilot is referenced as an industry-wide example. Sources: https://www.safebreach.com/blog/gemini-voice-assistant-prompt-injection-exploit/, https://www.miggo.io/post/weaponizing-calendar-invites-a-semantic-attack-on-google-gemini, https://arxiv.org/html/2505.14534v1
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Mitigations are delivered as continuous, layered rollouts rather than a single patch. Google has deployed: adversarial model training for Gemini 2.5, prompt injection classifiers, security thought reinforcement, markdown/URL sanitization with Safe Browsing, and user confirmation HITL framework in the Gemini app and Gemini in Workspace apps. Additionally, Google has patched specific pinpoint issues: (1) Gemini CLI prompt-injection flaw (CVE-2025-XXXX) fixed in v0.1.14 on July 25, 2025; (2) SafeBreach Gemini Voice Assistant notifications issue mitigated November 14, 2025; (3) Miggo Calendar-injection issue addressed. Administrator guidance: https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of Gemini's indirect prompt injection vulnerability has been publicly reported. Security researchers (SafeBreach, Miggo) have disclosed PoCs demonstrating the feasibility of attacks, and Unit 42 has documented web-based indirect prompt injection attacks in the wild against AI agents generally, but no specific Gemini exploitation campaign by a named APT or ransomware group has been publicly attributed as of July 2026. Related: CVE-2025-32711 (EchoLeak) affecting Microsoft 365 Copilot was disclosed with a CVSS 9.3 score, but this does not affect Gemini directly.
enrichment.mitigation: Google recommends a layered defense strategy: (1) Model training & hardening with adversarial data for Gemini 2.5 models; (2) Prompt injection content classifiers (ML-based detectors) that flag potentially malicious content; (3) Security thought reinforcement - system instructions telling Gemini to ignore instructions in untrusted data; (4) Markdown sanitization and URL redaction using Google Safe Browsing to block exfiltration paths; (5) User Confirmation Framework (Human-In-The-Loop) requiring explicit approval before sensitive tool calls; (6) End-user security mitigation notifications when prompt injection is blocked; (7) Heuristics such as Attention Tracker, spotlighting (control-token interleaving), paraphrasing, and self-reflection perplexity checks. Administrator guidance: https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini

findings.title: Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System
findings.summary: Executive summary People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors are targeting networks globally, including, but not limited to, telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. While these actors focus on large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers, they also leverage compromised devices and trusted connections to pivot into other networks. These actors often modify routers to maintain persistent, long-term access to networks. This activity partially overlaps with cyber threat actor reporting by the cybersecurity industry—commonly referred to as Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor, among others. The authoring agencies are not adopting a particular commercial naming convention and hereafter refer to those responsible for the cyber threat activity more generically as “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors” throughout this advisory. This cluster of cyber threat activity has been observed in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and other areas globally. This Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) includes observations from various government and industry investigations where the APT actors targeted internal enterprise environments, as well as systems and networks that deliver services directly to customers. This CSA details the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) leveraged by these APT actors to facilitate detection and threat hunting, and provides mitigation guidance to reduce the risk from these APT actors and their TTPs. This CSA is being released by the following authoring and co-sealing agencies: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United States Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) Australian Signals Directorate’s Austra

enrichment.technical_details: This is a CISA Threat Activity Group (TAG) / Joint advisory rather than a single-vulnerability bulletin. PRC state-sponsored APTs chain publicly known (N-day) vulnerabilities in internet-facing network edge devices against backbone/PE/CE routers, network gateways (Cisco, Ivanti Connect/Policy Secure, Palo Alto PAN-OS, etc.). Typical kill chain: (1) initial access by exploiting a web-UI authentication bypass such as CVE-2023-20198 (Cisco IOS XE) to create a local privilege-15 account, or by exploiting Smart Install (CVE-2018-0171) or Ivanti/PAN-OS GlobalProtect command injection (CVE-2023-46805+CVE-2024-21887 / CVE-2024-3400); (2) local privilege escalation via CVE-2023-20273 post-authentication command injection (Cisco IOS XE) to obtain root; (3) persistent, long-term access by modifying Access Control Lists (often adding an 'access-list 20' with attacker-controlled IPs), enabling GuestShell containers, installing SparrowDoor/ShadowPad backdoors (DLL side-loading) and unauthorized local accounts, and adding GRE/IPsec tunnels to foreign infrastructure; (4) credential capture from TACACS+/RADIUS traffic; (5) lateral pivot from compromised devices into adjacent networks using trusted connections. Cisco Talos attributes Salt Typhoon initial access to abuse of legitimate credentials on Cisco devices rather than novel zero-days.
enrichment.affected_products: Cisco IOS XE Software Web UI (CVE-2023-20198 + CVE-2023-20273 chain) — all versions with HTTP/HTTPS server enabled; Cisco IOS / IOS XE Software with Smart Install feature enabled (CVE-2018-0171); Ivanti Connect Secure 9.x and 22.x, Ivanti Policy Secure 9.x and 22.x (CVE-2023-46805 / CVE-2024-21887); Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS with GlobalProtect feature enabled — affected versions include PAN-OS 10.2, 11.0, 11.1, and 11.2 prior to vendor hotfixes (CVE-2024-3400)
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H (CVE-2023-20198 — the primary exploited CVE in the CISA advisory, per NVD/Cisco Security Advisory cisco-sa-iosxe-webui-privesc-j22SaA4z)
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public PoC and weaponized exploits are available for the underlying CVEs cited in Appendix B of AA25-239A. Examples include: https://github.com/smokeintheshell/CVE-2023-20198 (Cisco IOS XE Web UI auth bypass PoC), https://github.com/retkoussa/CVE-2024-3400 (Palo Alto PAN-OS command-injection PoC), and the watchTowr technical writeup/PoC for CVE-2024-3400 (Apr 16 2024). GreyNoise observed real-time exploitation of CVE-2023-20198 from ~110 malicious IPs (Bulgaria, Brazil, Singapore) and of CVE-2018-0171 in Dec 2024–Jan 2025 from IPs in Switzerland and the United States.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Vendor patches have been released for every underlying CVE in Appendix B of AA25-239A. Cisco patch: https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security/center/content/CiscoSecurityAdvisory/cisco-sa-iosxe-webui-privesc-j22SaA4z (covers CVE-2023-20198 and CVE-2023-20273 — first published Oct 16, 2023). Palo Alto Networks patch: https://security.paloaltonetworks.com/CVE-2024-3400 (hotfixes released Apr 2024 for PAN-OS 10.2, 11.0, 11.1, 11.2). Ivanti released patches for CVE-2023-46805 (Jan 2024) and CVE-2024-21887 (Jan/Feb 2024) for Connect Secure 9.x and 22.x and Policy Secure. Cisco Smart Install vulnerability CVE-2018-0171 is patched in affected IOS/IOS XE releases. AA25-239A itself does not introduce a new vulnerability — it documents already-patchable issues being actively abused and provides detection/hardening guidance.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Confirmed active in-the-wild exploitation. Per CISA AA25-239A (published 25 Aug 2025) and supporting reporting by multiple partners: Salt Typhoon (and related PRC APT activity) has been observed actively targeting backbone routers of major telecommunications providers as well as provider-edge and customer-edge routers; deployments have hit at least 200 organizations across 80 countries. Specific evidence: (1) Cisco Talos (Feb 2025) confirmed Salt Typhoon gained initial access to telecoms primarily via abuse of legitimate login credentials on Cisco devices; (2) Eclypsium (Jun 2025) confirmed Salt Typhoon access to Canadian telcos and Viasat using the CVE-2023-20198 + CVE-2023-20273 chain; (3) GreyNoise observed real-world exploitation of CVE-2018-0171 in Dec 2024–Jan 2025 and of CVE-2023-20198 from ~110 malicious IPs primarily in Bulgaria, Brazil, and Singapore (Feb 2025); (4) Multiple law-enforcement and intelligence reporting sources confirm Salt Typhoon operations against AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile, Charter, Lumen and other US carriers. The advisory explicitly states that 'exploitation of zero-day vulnerabilities has not been observed to date' — the activity relies on exploitation of KNOWN (N-day) vulnerabilities against unpatched or poorly hardened devices.
enrichment.threat_actors: Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, GhostEmperor (PRC state-sponsored APTs; also tracked as Earth Estries, FamousSparrow, and other aliases by various vendors)
enrichment.mitigation: Per CISA/JCDC guidance in AA25-239A: (1) Immediately patch all internet-facing edge devices for the CVEs listed in Appendix B of the advisory and the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog — prioritize Cisco IOS XE Web UI (CVE-2023-20198/CVE-2023-20273), Cisco Smart Install (CVE-2018-0171), Ivanti Connect/Policy Secure (CVE-2024-21887 / CVE-2023-46805), Palo Alto PAN-OS GlobalProtect (CVE-2024-3400); (2) Pull running network-equipment configurations and compare against an authorized golden baseline; review ACLs (look for additions such as 'access-list 20'), TACACS+/RADIUS/AAA configs, packet-capture/SPAN settings, and virtual-container/GuestShell instances for unauthorized changes; (3) Disable unused network services and features — Cisco Smart Install, the HTTP/HTTPS server if not required, GuestShell where unnecessary (use 'guestshell disable' / 'guestshell destroy'); restrict SNMP write access; (4) Replace weak/legacy credentials (e.g., cisco/cisco) and disable Type-5/7 password hashing; use SSH on non-default ports with strict authorized-keys management; (5) Monitor for unexpected GRE / IPsec tunnels, especially to foreign infrastructure, and unexpected external IPs in AAA server configs; (6) Implement robust change management that stores, tracks, and periodically audits all device configurations; (7) Capture and analyse TACACS+/RADIUS traffic to prevent credential theft; (8) Verify exposure with NIST SP 800-115 / CISA technical assessments plus continuous attack-emulation (AttackIQ, Picus, etc.) mapped to MITRE ATT&CK TTPs documented in the advisory.

findings.title: Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies
findings.summary: Executive Summary This joint cybersecurity advisory (CSA) highlights a Russian state-sponsored cyber campaign targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies. This includes those involved in the coordination, transport, and delivery of foreign assistance to Ukraine. Since 2022, Western logistics entities and IT companies have faced an elevated risk of targeting by the Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165—tracked in the cybersecurity community under several names (see “ Cybersecurity Industry Tracking ”). The actors’ cyber espionage-oriented campaign, targeting technology companies and logistics entities, uses a mix of previously disclosed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). The authoring agencies expect similar targeting and TTP use to continue. Executives and network defenders at logistics entities and technology companies should recognize the elevated threat of unit 26165 targeting, increase monitoring and threat hunting for known TTPs and indicators of compromise (IOCs), and posture network defenses with a presumption of targeting. This cyber espionage-oriented campaign targeting logistics entities and technology companies uses a mix of previously disclosed TTPs and is likely connected to these actors’ wide scale targeting of IP cameras in Ukraine and bordering NATO nations. The following authors and co-sealers are releasing this CSA: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United Kingdom National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) Germany Federal Intelligence Service (BND) Bundesnachrichtendienst Germany Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik Germany Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution (BfV) Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz Czech Republic Military Intelligence (VZ) Vojenské zpravodajství Czech Republic National Cyber and Infor

enrichment.technical_details: AA25-141A documents a multi-year (since 2022) Russian state-sponsored espionage campaign by GRU Unit 26165 against Western logistics entities and IT companies supporting aid to Ukraine. Initial access via spearphishing [T1566] and credential brute-force [T1110], followed by exploitation of Microsoft Outlook (CVE-2023-23397 — EoP via crafted PidLidReminderFileParameter triggering outbound SMB NTLMv2 hash leakage), RARLAB WinRAR before 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831 — code execution via spoofed benign file inside a ZIP archive), and Roundcube Webmail (CVE-2020-12641 command injection in rcube_image.php, plus CVE-2020-35730 and CVE-2021-44026 SQL injection / authenticated RCE). Follow-on activity: corporate VPN exploitation [T1133] using public vulnerabilities and SQL injection [T1190], Exchange mailbox permission abuse, RDP lateral movement [T1021.001], and exfiltration of mail via periodic EWS/IMAP queries [T1114.002, T1119, T1029] after archiving data as .zip [T1560.001] exfiltrated through a dropped OpenSSH binary [T1048]. Custom tooling: HEADLACE (spearphishing/credential-theft dropper), MASEPIE (Python RAT), OCEANMAP, and STEELHOOK. The actor also performed credential brute force and RTSP reconnaissance against Hikvision and other IP cameras at Ukrainian border crossings and logistics hubs, and exploited a Hikvision backdoor string ('YWRtaW46MTEK') to obtain camera feeds [T1125].
enrichment.affected_products: Microsoft Outlook (CVE-2023-23397); RARLAB WinRAR before 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831); Roundcube Webmail before 1.4.4 (CVE-2020-12641); Roundcube Webmail (CVE-2020-35730); Roundcube Webmail 1.3.0–1.4.11 (CVE-2021-44026); Microsoft Exchange Server (mailbox permission tampering, EWS/IMAP abuse); Hikvision and other Internet-connected IP cameras at border crossings; SOHO routers/proxying devices; Industrial Control System (ICS) components used for railway management; corporate VPN infrastructure.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public proof-of-concept exploit code exists on GitHub for CVE-2023-23397 (https://github.com/Trackflaw/CVE-2023-23397), CVE-2020-12641 (https://github.com/mbadanoiu/CVE-2020-12641), and CVE-2023-38831 (https://github.com/HDCE-inc/CVE-2023-38831). APT28/GRU Unit 26165 also weaponized these exploits against real targets in this campaign, as documented in CISA Advisory AA25-141A.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — official vendor patches exist for all referenced CVEs. Microsoft Outlook patch released March 14, 2023 (CVE-2023-23397). RARLAB WinRAR 6.23 released August 2023 (CVE-2023-38831). Roundcube Webmail 1.4.4 and follow-on security releases address CVE-2020-12641, CVE-2020-35730, and CVE-2021-44026. Advisory index: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a and joint PDF at https://media.defense.gov/2025/May/21/2003719846/-1/-1/0/CSA_RUSSIAN_GRU_TARGET_LOGISTICS.PDF.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active in-the-wild exploitation by Russian GRU Unit 26165 since 2022. Sources: CISA Cybersecurity Advisory AA25-141A (jointly issued by CISA, NSA, FBI, the Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center, and international partners including agencies from the UK, Germany, France, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the Netherlands, Czechia, Poland, Estonia, and Latvia; last revised April 17, 2026); the joint advisory PDF (https://media.defense.gov/2025/May/21/2003719846/-1/-1/0/CSA_RUSSIAN_GRU_TARGET_LOGISTICS.PDF); and corroborating industry coverage from SafeBreach, AttackIQ, and SocPrime.
enrichment.threat_actors: Russian GRU 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165 — also tracked in the cybersecurity community as APT28, Fancy Bear, Forest Blizzard, BlueDelta, STRONTIUM, Sednit, and Sofacy.
enrichment.mitigation: Patch all referenced CVEs: Microsoft Outlook against CVE-2023-23397, RARLAB WinRAR to version 6.23 or later, and Roundcube Webmail to a patched release covering CVE-2020-12641, CVE-2020-35730, and CVE-2021-44026. Use Microsoft's published script to search Exchange mailboxes for malicious calendar items related to CVE-2023-23397. Harden IP cameras: ensure devices are vendor-supported, apply all firmware/security patches, replace unsupported units, disable remote access when unnecessary, place cameras behind a firewall/allowlist, require a VPN plus MFA for management access, disable UPnP/P2P/Anonymous Visit, close unused ports and services (e.g., FTP, web UI), enable authenticated RTSP only, audit camera accounts, and configure/monitor camera logging. SOHO routers and devices should be hardened identically (UPnP off, default credentials changed, firmware updated) and blocked from traffic that should originate at enterprise endpoints. Apply least-privilege Exchange mailbox permissions, monitor EWS/IMAP and RDP for anomalous use, enable MFA on all VPN accounts, patch VPN appliances, and segment OT/ICS networks used for railway management. Implement robust logging, network segmentation, and the full D3-* control set enumerated in the advisory (D3-SU, D3-ITF, D3-NAM, D3-AA, D3-ET, D3-MFA, D3-NI, D3-ACH, D3-UBA, D3-UAP).

findings.title: Browser Security: Zero-Days Are Only Part of the Problem

enrichment.technical_details: The post argues browser security risk extends beyond zero-day RCE in V8/Blink/Skia to include: (a) rendering-logic and JavaScript-engine memory-safety defects enabling type confusion or out-of-bounds read/write; (b) sandbox-escape chains that pivot from a browser renderer to underlying OS; (c) abuse of legitimate deployment frameworks such as Microsoft's ClickOnce to deliver signed, trusted-by-default payloads; (d) adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) reverse-proxy phishing that bypasses MFA and harvests live session tokens; (e) malicious browser extensions and HTML smuggling that bypass network controls; (f) web-delivered social engineering, clickjacking and cross-site scripting; and (g) AI-driven data exfiltration where the browser mediates access to corporate LLM prompts, source code and SaaS data.
enrichment.affected_products: Chromium-based web browsers (Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, Brave, Opera, Vivaldi, Arc) and shared Chromium open-source components (Blink, V8 JavaScript engine, Skia renderer); Microsoft ClickOnce deployment technology (.NET Framework) as an actively abused Windows attack surface. No specific version numbers are named in the article.
enrichment.exploit_available: Public proof-of-concept code is referenced as the driving factor for widespread N-day exploitation of unpatched Chromium-based browsers. Weaponized in-the-wild exploitation has been confirmed by Google for the adjacent 2026 Chromium V8 zero-days (e.g., CVE-2026-2441, CVE-2026-3909, CVE-2026-3910, CVE-2026-5281, CVE-2026-11645, and CVE-2026-1861/Libvpx). The post does not link a single specific PoC; broader exploitation landscape documented at https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/google-patches-fifth-chrome-zero-day.html and https://socprime.com/blog/cve-2026-3910-vulnerability/ .
enrichment.patch_available: Patches are continuously released by Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, Mozilla Firefox, and Apple Safari for the Chromium-based zero-days discussed in adjacent 2026 reporting (e.g., Chrome 146.0.7680.75 for CVE-2026-3910, Chrome emergency update for CVE-2026-11645 on Jun 9, 2026). However, the post's central thesis is that patching alone is insufficient because large numbers of organizations run outdated/unpatched browsers; CrowdStrike's 2026 Global Threat Report notes 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure, meaning patches often arrive after initial exploitation.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation is the post's foundational premise. Quoted data points from the article: (1) Verizon 2026 DBIR — vulnerability exploitation surpassed stolen credentials as the top breach entry point, accounting for roughly 31% of breaches (up from ~20% the prior year); (2) CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report — 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure (zero-day window). Examples of actively exploited in-the-wild browser bugs from 2026 that contextualize the post: CVE-2026-2441 (Feb 2026, Chrome V8), CVE-2026-3909, CVE-2026-3910 (Mar 2026, Chrome V8), CVE-2026-5281, CVE-2026-11645 (Jun 2026, Chrome V8), and CVE-2026-1861 (Libvpx) — Google has confirmed in-the-wild exploitation for each, though attribution to specific threat actors remains undisclosed by Google.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The post is a thematic/thought-leadership piece and does not name any specific threat actor, APT group, ransomware operator, or campaign exploiting the browser vulnerabilities it discusses.
enrichment.mitigation: (1) Enable and rapidly deploy Chrome/Chromium and Microsoft .NET/ClickOnce security updates; (2) Deploy CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access as a lightweight browser runtime monitor that uses JavaScript Language Randomization (JSLR) — a moving-target-defense that randomizes JS runtime tokens each session to break N-day and zero-day exploit chains; (3) Enforce network and endpoint controls that block phishing, AiTM frameworks, session-hijack indicators and malicious extensions; (4) Restrict ClickOnce to publisher-allowlist/trusted-deployment-policy and monitor for masquerade signatures (see CrowdStrike Part-2 ClickOnce blog); (5) Use browser-isolation or managed-browser profiles for unmanaged/BYOD endpoints; (6) Segment identity and credential access so a browser session-token compromise does not yield broad access.

findings.title: 94% of Organizations Report Cloud Breaches: CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey

enrichment.technical_details: Technical details unavailable. This is not a vulnerability advisory. The article instead summarizes findings of the CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey: 94% of surveyed organizations experienced cloud intrusions resulting in data exposure or exfiltration; 73% cannot consistently detect cloud intrusions; 95% lack full sensor/agent coverage of cloud workloads; 78% cannot reliably distinguish legitimate from malicious cloud activity; 79% say their tools generate too many alerts; 77% of triage time is spent on false positives; 98% do not remediate every identified threat; 68% take 15+ minutes to detect a cloud intrusion (51% require ≥1 hour); 91% cannot contain cloud intrusions in real time; 83% run AI/ML workloads in the cloud and 81% of those have experienced AI/ML-targeted incidents; 38% of breached organizations cite tool fragmentation as a contributing factor. Common observed attack themes include abuse of legitimate identities, APIs, and cloud services, and fragmented visibility across the cloud control plane and workloads.
enrichment.exploit_available: Not applicable. No CVE, vulnerability, exploit, or proof-of-concept is documented in this blog post.
enrichment.patch_available: Not applicable. No vendor security patch is associated with this item — the blog is a research/survey report, not a vulnerability bulletin, and no CVE or fixed-software identifier is referenced.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No specific in-the-wild exploitation of a named vulnerability is reported in this post. The '94% of organizations report cloud breaches' figure refers to self-reported survey statistics about general cloud intrusions and data exposure/exfiltration, rather than confirmed exploitation of any specific CVE. No named threat actor, APT campaign, ransomware operator, or targeted vulnerability exploitation campaign is documented in the source.
enrichment.mitigation: Implement Cloud Detection and Response (CDR) by: (1) achieving unified visibility across cloud workloads, identities, APIs, and cloud control-plane activity; (2) correlating activity across both the cloud control plane and workload layers; (3) integrating cloud detections into SOC/SIEM workflows to reduce alert noise and operational friction; and (4) reducing reliance on manual investigation. CrowdStrike Falcon Cloud Security (part of the CrowdStrike Falcon Platform) is cited as the vendor's CDR solution.

findings.title: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
findings.summary: Posted by Jiacheng Lu, Software Engineer, Google Pixel Team Google is continuously advancing the security of Pixel devices. We have been focusing on hardening the cellular baseband modem against exploitation. Recognizing the risks associated within the complex modem firmware, Pixel 9 shipped with mitigations against a range of memory-safety vulnerabilities. For Pixel 10, Google is advancing its proactive security measures further. Following our previous discussion on "Deploying Rust in Existing Firmware Codebases" , this post shares a concrete application: integrating a memory-safe Rust DNS(Domain Name System) parser into the modem firmware. The new Rust-based DNS parser significantly reduces our security risk by mitigating an entire class of vulnerabilities in a risky area, while also laying the foundation for broader adoption of memory-safe code in other areas. Here we share our experience of working on it, and hope it can inspire the use of more memory safe languages in low-level environments. Why Modem Memory Safety Can’t Wait In recent years, we have seen increasing interest in the cellular modem from attackers and security researchers. For example, Google's Project Zero gained remote code execution on Pixel modems over the Internet. Pixel modem has tens of Megabytes of executable code. Given the complexity and remote attack surface of the modem, other critical memory safety vulnerabilities may remain in the predominantly memory-unsafe firmware code. Why DNS? The DNS protocol is most commonly known in the context of browsers finding websites. With the evolution of cellular technology, modern cellular communications have migrated to digital data networks; consequently, even basic operations such as call forwarding rely on DNS services. DNS is a complex protocol and requires parsing of untrusted data, which can lead to vulnerabilities, particularly when implemented in a memory-unsafe language (example: CVE-2024-27227 ).
